abstract: Although the green revolution adapted a handful of crops to homogeneous and high-input industrialized agriculture, much of the global population still relies on the local production of variable crop cultivars by low-input smallholder farms. This diversity of unhomogenized crops offers raw materials for genetic gain and cultivar improvement. Here, we constructed a 33-member pangenome reference and a diversity panel across 1,984 cultivars and landraces. Specifically, our analyses demonstrated multiple nested and deeply diverged structural variants in the domestication gene SHATTERING1. As proof of concept for pangenome-accelerated trait discovery, we connected biosynthetic gene cluster structural variation to phenotypic leaf concentration of the cyanogenic glucoside dhurrin. Combined, these approaches will accelerate breeding and trait discovery and provide a framework for similar applications in other crops.
notes:
## Key Findings

- 33-member sorghum pangenome + 1,984 cultivar/landrace diversity panel constructed.
- Multiple nested, deeply diverged SVs found in domestication gene SHATTERING1.
- Biosynthetic gene cluster SVs linked to dhurrin leaf concentration — proof-of-concept for pangenome-accelerated trait discovery.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Graph-based pangenome construction from diverse sorghum cultivars and landraces.
- SV-GWAS connecting pangenome structural variation to quantitative phenotypes.

title: Population-level structural variant characterization using pangenome graphs
abstract: Population-level structural variant (SV) profiling is crucial in the era of pangenomes. Here we present Swave, a sequence-to-image, deep learning-based method that accurately resolves both simple and complex SVs from assembly-derived pangenome graphs. Swave introduces 'projection waves' to summarize dotplot images capturing mapping patterns between reference and SV alleles. A recurrent neural network distinguishes true SV signals from background noise. When applied to healthy and rare-disease cohorts, Swave reveals complex and polymorphic SV patterns across human populations and identifies potentially pathogenic SVs, facilitating the creation of comprehensive population-level SV catalogs.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Swave: sequence-to-image deep learning method resolves simple and complex SVs from pangenome graphs.
- 'Projection waves' summarize dotplot images of mapping patterns between reference and SV alleles.
- RNN distinguishes true SV signals from background noise; applied to healthy and rare-disease cohorts.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Converts SV detection to an image classification problem using dotplot projections.
- Recurrent neural network applied to pangenome-derived sequence-to-image representations.

title: Predicting disease-specific histone modifications and functional effects of non-coding variants by leveraging DNA language models
abstract: The authors develop a novel large language model-based deep learning framework tailored for disease-contextual prediction of histone modifications and variant effects. Using Alzheimer's disease as a case study, they integrate epigenomic data from multiple patient samples to construct a comprehensive, disease-specific histone modification dataset. Their model incorporates a Mixture of Experts architecture to distinguish between disease and healthy epigenetic states, demonstrating robust performance and providing insights into disease-associated genetic variants and pathways.
notes:
## Key Findings

- LLM-based Mixture of Experts framework for disease-contextual histone modification prediction.
- Integrates patient epigenomic data to distinguish disease vs. healthy epigenetic states.
- Applied to variant effect prediction in a disease-specific context (Alzheimer's disease).

## Methods Worth Noting

- Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture applied to DNA language model outputs.
- Epigenomic dataset constructed from multiple patient samples for training.

title: Pangenome analysis reveals families of ubiquitin-ligase adaptors as key genomic divergence drivers that lead to hybrid incompatibility
abstract: This pangenome study analyzes populations from multiple species, revealing that genomic divergence is primarily driven by large interspecific unaligned regions. The research identifies specific families of ubiquitin-ligase adaptors as key drivers of genomic divergence, contributing to our understanding of hybrid incompatibility and genome evolution through a pangenomic lens.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Pangenome analysis reveals ubiquitin-ligase adaptor gene families as primary drivers of interspecific genomic divergence.
- Large interspecific unaligned regions are the dominant source of genomic divergence.
- Hybrid incompatibility linked to accumulation of these divergence-driving gene families.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Multi-species pangenome construction to identify unaligned/divergent regions.
- Comparative genomics across population samples from multiple species.

title: Automated interpretable artificial intelligence genomic prediction with AIGP
abstract: The paper introduces Artificial Intelligence Genomic Prediction (AIGP), an open-source, end-to-end toolkit designed for genomic prediction research. AIGP aims to provide both automated performance and interpretability, addressing the 'black box' nature of traditional deep learning models in genomics by incorporating architectures that allow for biological interpretation of the prediction process.
notes:
## Key Findings

- AIGP: open-source end-to-end toolkit for interpretable AI genomic prediction.
- Addresses the 'black box' problem in deep learning for genomics via interpretable architectures.
- Automated performance with biological interpretation of prediction.

## Methods Worth Noting

- End-to-end toolkit design for genomic prediction research.
- Incorporates architectures enabling biological interpretation alongside high prediction performance.

title: ADAR-GPT: Learning the language of the epitranscriptome
abstract: The study introduces ADAR-GPT, a framework that adapts GPT-class language models to the field of epitranscriptomics. By treating RNA sequences and their modifications as a biological language, the model learns to identify and classify A-to-I RNA editing sites with high accuracy across different tissues and species, demonstrating the power of language modeling in understanding complex biological sequences.
notes:
## Key Findings

- ADAR-GPT: GPT-class language model applied to epitranscriptomics for A-to-I RNA editing site classification.
- Treats RNA sequences + modifications as a biological language; high accuracy across tissues and species.
- Extends biological LM paradigm beyond DNA to RNA modifications.

## Methods Worth Noting

- GPT-class architecture adapted to RNA sequence + modification inputs.
- Trained and evaluated across multiple tissues and species for cross-context generalization.

title: Many roads lead to a plant cistrome: mapping and interpreting transcription factor binding in plants
abstract: This comprehensive review examines the current strategies and challenges in mapping the cistromes of plant species. It discusses high-throughput methods for identifying transcription factor binding sites and the computational challenges in interpreting these regulatory elements across diverse plant species, highlighting the importance of cistromics in crop improvement and plant biology.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Comprehensive review of strategies for mapping plant TF cistromes across species.
- Highlights computational challenges in interpreting regulatory elements across diverse plant genomes.
- Discusses implications for crop improvement and functional plant biology.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Review of high-throughput methods: ChIP-seq, DAP-seq, ATAC-seq, CUT&RUN in plant systems.
- Comparative cistromics across species to identify conserved regulatory elements.

title: GE-BiCross: A Hierarchical Bidirectional Cross-Attention Framework for Genotype-by-Environment Prediction in Maize
abstract: Genotype-by-environment interactions are central to crop adaptation and yield stability. We developed GE-BiCross, a hierarchical bidirectional cross-attention framework for maize prediction, which consistently outperformed machine learning and deep learning baselines across six agronomic traits using a large-scale dataset of 360,000 observations.
notes:
## Key Findings

- GE-BiCross: hierarchical bidirectional cross-attention framework for maize GxE prediction.
- Outperforms ML and DL baselines across 6 agronomic traits on 360,000 observations.
- Bidirectional attention captures complex genotype-environment interaction patterns.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Hierarchical bidirectional cross-attention architecture for joint genotype-environment modeling.
- Large-scale dataset of 360,000 observations across diverse environments.

title: HitAnno: Atlas-level cell type annotation based on scATAC-seq data via a hierarchical language model
abstract: HitAnno is a hierarchical language model for accurate and scalable cell type annotation in scATAC-seq data. It employs a two-level attention mechanism that captures accessibility profiles hierarchically, revealing co-accessibility patterns among peaks.
notes:
## Key Findings

- HitAnno: hierarchical language model for atlas-level cell type annotation from scATAC-seq.
- Two-level attention captures co-accessibility patterns among chromatin peaks.
- Scalable to atlas-level datasets.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Hierarchical language model with two-level attention for chromatin accessibility profiles.
- Applied to large-scale scATAC-seq datasets for cell type annotation.

title: A DNA foundation model predicts osteoporosis risk genes without proximity bias
abstract: Rosalind is a DNA foundation model fine-tuned on human genetic variation that predicts variant-gene regulatory relationships from sequence without relying on nearest-gene heuristics. It demonstrates high accuracy in osteoporosis risk gene prediction.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Rosalind: DNA foundation model fine-tuned on human genetic variation for variant-gene regulatory prediction.
- Predicts regulatory relationships from sequence alone, without nearest-gene proximity heuristics.
- High accuracy demonstrated for osteoporosis risk gene prediction.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Fine-tuning of DNA foundation model on human genetic variation data.
- Sequence-only prediction approach that bypasses proximity bias inherent in GWAS follow-up.

title: CDS-BART: A BART-Based Foundation Model for mRNA Sequence Analysis
abstract: Proposed CDS-BART is a foundation model for mRNA sequence analysis that integrates SentencePiece tokenization with BART training. It was pre-trained on mRNA data from nine taxonomic groups to learn codon usage and mRNA structure.
notes:
## Key Findings

- CDS-BART: BART-style foundation model pre-trained on mRNA data from nine taxonomic groups.
- Learns codon usage patterns and mRNA structural features across organisms.
- SentencePiece tokenization integrated with BART encoder-decoder training.

## Methods Worth Noting

- BART encoder-decoder architecture adapted for biological sequence modeling.
- Multi-taxon pre-training enables cross-species mRNA analysis.

title: FishMamba-1: A Linear-Complexity Foundation Model for Deciphering Polyploid Cyprinid Genomes
abstract: FishMamba is the first genomic foundation model tailored for polyploid genomes, built on the Mamba-2 architecture. It processes context windows of 32kb, enabling the modeling of distal regulatory patterns in expanded genomes.
notes:
## Key Findings

- FishMamba-1: first genomic foundation model specifically designed for polyploid genomes, built on Mamba-2.
- 32kb context window enables modeling of distal regulatory elements in expanded polyploid genomes.
- Linear-complexity architecture scales efficiently to large, repetitive polyploid genomes.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Mamba-2 state space model architecture for linear-complexity long-range sequence modeling.
- Applied to cyprinid fish polyploid genomes; directly translatable to plant polyploidy contexts.

title: Benchmarking DNA Foundation Models: Biological Blind Spots in Evo2 Variant-Effect Prediction
abstract: Evaluated Evo2 on controlled benchmarks for variant-effect prediction, identifying systematic blind spots in short-range biological signals and sensitivity to neutral contextual features, challenging claims of zero-shot pathogenicity prediction.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Systematic evaluation of Evo2 on controlled benchmarks for variant-effect prediction.
- Identifies blind spots in short-range biological signals; sensitive to neutral contextual features.
- Challenges zero-shot pathogenicity prediction claims for current DNA foundation models.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Controlled benchmark design to isolate specific capabilities and failure modes of DNA FMs.
- Systematic analysis of sensitivity to biological vs. neutral sequence context.

title: Uncovering genetic mechanisms underlying trait variation in switchgrass using explainable artificial intelligence
abstract: Integrated SNPs and RNA-seq data with machine learning models in switchgrass to predict trait plasticity. Explainable AI methods recovered canonical flowering regulators and revealed novel environment-specific candidate genes.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Integrated SNPs + RNA-seq with ML in switchgrass to predict trait plasticity.
- Explainable AI recovers canonical flowering regulators (e.g., photoperiod genes).
- Reveals novel environment-specific candidate genes not identified by traditional GWAS.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Multi-omic integration: SNP genotypes + transcriptome data for ML-based trait prediction.
- Explainable AI (XAI) applied to identify causal regulatory candidates.

title: CollapsedChrom: resolving the assembly of collapsed chromosomal segments in polyploid genomes of the model grass genus Brachypodium
abstract: Developed a depth-sensitive curation strategy (CollapsedChrom) to detect and rescue collapsed regions in polyploid Brachypodium genomes, capturing the full genomic architecture of these perennial grasses.
notes:
## Key Findings

- CollapsedChrom: depth-sensitive strategy to detect and rescue collapsed chromosomal segments in polyploid plant genomes.
- Applied to Brachypodium polyploid species to capture full genomic architecture.
- Rescues previously missed genomic sequences in repetitive/collapsed regions.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Sequencing depth analysis to identify collapsed vs. properly assembled regions.
- Curation pipeline that recovers collapsed segments and restores genomic completeness.

title: The Genomic Legacy of Ancient Polyploidy in Crop Domestication
abstract: Tested whether paleologs from ancient whole-genome duplications are enriched in domestication gene lists across 22 crop species. Found that purifying selection on copy number does not preclude functional selection.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Tests enrichment of ancient WGD paleologs in domestication gene lists across 22 crop species.
- Purifying selection on copy number does not preclude subsequent functional selection on retained duplicates.
- Links ancient polyploidy evolutionary history directly to crop domestication.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Cross-species comparative genomics across 22 domesticated crop species.
- Integration of WGD paleolog databases with crop domestication gene lists.

title: SNPgen: Phenotype-Supervised Genotype Representation and Synthetic Data Generation via Latent Diffusion
abstract: SNPgen is a two-stage conditional latent diffusion framework for generating phenotype-supervised synthetic genotypes. It matches real-data predictive performance for complex diseases while ensuring privacy preservation.
notes:
## Key Findings

- SNPgen: two-stage conditional latent diffusion for phenotype-supervised synthetic genotype generation.
- Matches real-data predictive performance for complex diseases while being privacy-preserving.
- Enables synthetic genomics data generation for downstream ML training.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Two-stage: latent encoding of genotypes + conditional diffusion for phenotype-guided generation.
- Privacy-preserving by design; synthetic data matches statistical properties of real genotypes.

title: Continuous Diffusion Transformers for Designing Synthetic Regulatory Elements
abstract: Presented a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) for generating 200bp cell-type-specific regulatory DNA sequences. It improves predicted regulatory activity by 38x using Enformer-based reinforcement learning.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Diffusion Transformer (DiT) generates 200bp cell-type-specific regulatory DNA sequences.
- 38× improvement in predicted regulatory activity vs. baseline using Enformer-based RL fine-tuning.
- Continuous diffusion in sequence space enables high-quality biological sequence design.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Continuous Diffusion Transformer architecture adapted for discrete DNA sequence space.
- Enformer-based reinforcement learning reward signal guides regulatory activity optimization.

title: Discovery of a Hematopoietic Manifold in scGPT Yields a Method for Extracting Performant Algorithms from Biological Foundation Model Internals
abstract: Discovery of a compact algorithm extracted from the scGPT foundation model internals using mechanistic interpretability. The extracted operator resolves into explicit gene programs without retraining.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Mechanistic interpretability applied to scGPT reveals a compact hematopoietic manifold in model internals.
- Extracted algorithm resolves into explicit gene programs without any retraining.
- Demonstrates that useful biology can be extracted directly from foundation model weights.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Mechanistic interpretability techniques (activation patching, probing) applied to scGPT.
- Algorithm extraction from model internals without fine-tuning or retraining.

title: Transposable Element Dynamics Drive Genome Size Variation along Environmental Gradients in Hordeum
abstract: A substantial variation in genome size has been observed among individuals of the same species. Theory predicts that increased genome size may confer an advantage in populations with small effective size. However, contradictory evidence for the correlation with environmental variation, and limited understanding of the underlying genetic mechanisms, has cast doubts on the adaptive role of genome size variation. To address this, we studied two Hordeum species which were collected at the same sites along a wide range of environments but differ in life habits (annual/perennial) and mating strategy (self/outcrossing). We detected substantial genome size variation, with differences of up to 10% in both species. While the mating system influenced the distribution of variation within populations, it did not alter the overall range of genome sizes. Drought emerged as the primary environmental factor associated with larger genomes, with past transposable elements (TE) bursts identified as the potential source of genome expansion. Genome-wide association analyses revealed that TE silencing is central to genome size regulation, with selection maintaining smaller genomes among individuals with higher fitness in favorable habitats. Under stressful conditions, silencing becomes less efficient, allowing larger genomes to persist and thereby increasing the reservoir of genetic variation available for selection. Together, these findings provide an integrative view of genome size dynamics in natural populations and underscore its exaptive role in maintenance of genetic variation under environmental stress.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Genome size varies up to 10% within *Hordeum* species, primarily driven by TE burst activity.
- Drought is the main environmental factor correlated with larger genome size.
- GWAS reveals TE silencing machinery as the central regulator of genome size; selection favors smaller genomes in favorable habitats but relaxes under stress, allowing TE-expanded genomes to persist.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Comparative study of annual/perennial and self/outcrossing *Hordeum* pairs sampled at the same sites for controlled environmental comparison.
- Genome-wide association study (GWAS) linking genome size to TE silencing gene variants.
- TE burst timing inferred from genomic analysis to link historical TE activity to current genome expansion.

title: Signatures of Soft Selective Sweeps Predominate in the Yellow Fever Mosquito Aedes aegypti
abstract: The Aedes aegypti mosquito is a vector for human arboviruses and zoonotic diseases and therefore poses a serious threat to public health. Understanding how Ae. aegypti adapts to environmental pressures-such as insecticides-is critical for developing effective mitigation strategies. However, most traditional methods for detecting recent positive selection search for signatures of classic 'hard' selective sweeps, and to date no studies have examined soft sweeps in Ae. aegypti. This is a significant limitation as this is vital information for understanding the pace of adaptation-populations that can immediately respond to new selective pressures are expected to adapt more often via standing variation or recurrent adaptive mutations (both of which may produce soft sweeps) than via de novo mutations (which produces hard sweeps). To this end, we used a machine learning method capable of detecting hard and soft sweeps to investigate positive selection in Ae. aegypti population samples from Africa and the Americas. Our results reveal that soft sweeps are significantly more common than hard sweeps, which may imply that this species can respond quickly to environmental stressors. This is a particularly concerning finding for vector control methods that aim to eradicate Ae. aegypti using insecticides. We highlight genes under selection that include both well-characterized and putatively novel insecticide resistance genes. These findings underscore the importance of using methods capable of detecting and distinguishing hard and soft sweeps, implicate soft sweeps as a major selective mode in Ae. aegypti, and highlight genes that may aid in the control of Ae. aegypti populations.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Soft selective sweeps (from standing variation or recurrent mutations) are significantly more common than hard sweeps in *Ae. aegypti*, suggesting this mosquito adaptss rapidly to new pressures like insecticides.
- ML method successfully distinguishes hard vs. soft sweep signatures genome-wide, identifying both known and novel candidate insecticide resistance genes.
- Findings have direct implications for vector control: insecticide eradication strategies may be undermined by soft-sweep-mediated rapid resistance evolution.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Used a machine learning classifier capable of distinguishing hard sweeps, soft sweeps, and neutral regions from population genomic data.
- Applied to population samples from Africa and the Americas to assess geographic variation in sweep mode.
- Methodological approach is broadly applicable for population genomics studies in any species, including crop plants.

title: Conservation of regulatory elements with highly diverged sequences across large evolutionary distances
abstract: Developmental gene expression is a remarkably conserved process, yet most cis-regulatory elements (CREs) lack sequence conservation, especially at larger evolutionary distances. Some evidence suggests that CREs at the same genomic position remain functionally conserved independent of sequence conservation. However, the extent of such positional conservation remains unclear. Here, we profiled the regulatory genome in mouse and chicken embryonic hearts at equivalent developmental stages and found that most CREs lack sequence conservation. To identify positionally conserved CREs, we introduced the synteny-based algorithm interspecies point projection, which identifies up to fivefold more orthologs than alignment-based approaches. We termed positionally conserved orthologs 'indirectly conserved' and showed that they exhibited chromatin signatures and sequence composition similar to sequence-conserved CREs but greater shuffling of transcription factor binding sites between orthologs. Finally, we validated indirectly conserved chicken enhancers using in vivo reporter assays in mouse. By overcoming alignment-based limitations, we revealed widespread functional conservation of sequence-divergent CREs.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Most cis-regulatory elements (CREs) lack sequence conservation across large evolutionary distances, yet developmental gene expression remains conserved.
- A novel synteny-based algorithm called 'interspecies point projection' identifies up to 5x more orthologous CREs than alignment-based approaches.
- Positionally conserved ('indirectly conserved') CREs share chromatin signatures with sequence-conserved CREs but show greater shuffling of transcription factor binding sites.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Profiling of regulatory genome in mouse and chicken embryonic hearts at equivalent developmental stages.
- Synteny-based interspecies point projection algorithm for identifying positionally conserved CRE orthologs.
- In vivo reporter assays in mouse to validate predicted conserved chicken enhancers.

title: The human and non-human primate developmental GTEx projects
abstract: Many human diseases are the result of early developmental defects. As most paediatric diseases and disorders are rare, children are critically underrepresented in research. Functional genomics studies primarily rely on adult tissues and lack critical cell states in specific developmental windows. In parallel, little is known about the conservation of developmental programmes across non-human primate (NHP) species, with implications for human evolution. Here we introduce the developmental Genotype-Tissue Expression (dGTEx) projects, which span humans and NHPs and aim to integrate gene expression, regulation and genetics data across development and species. The dGTEx cohort will consist of 74 tissue sites across 120 human donors from birth to adulthood, and developmentally matched NHP age groups, with additional prenatal and adult animals, with 126 rhesus macaques (Macaca mulatta) and 72 common marmosets (Callithrix jacchus). The data will comprise whole-genome sequencing, extensive bulk, single-cell and spatial gene expression profiles, and chromatin accessibility data across tissues and development. Through community engagement and donor diversity, the human dGTEx study seeks to address disparities in genomic research. Thus, dGTEx will provide a reference human and NHP dataset and tissue bank, enabling research into developmental changes in expression and gene regulation, childhood disorders and the effect of genetic variation on development.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Introduces the developmental GTEx (dGTEx) project spanning humans and non-human primates (NHPs) to study developmental gene expression and regulation.
- Cohort includes 120 human donors (birth to adulthood) across 74 tissue sites, plus 126 rhesus macaques and 72 common marmosets at matched developmental stages.
- Will integrate WGS, bulk/single-cell/spatial transcriptomics, and chromatin accessibility data across development.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Multi-species comparative approach: human, rhesus macaque, and common marmoset developmental cohorts.
- Combined multimodal genomics: WGS, bulk RNA-seq, scRNA-seq, spatial transcriptomics, and ATAC-seq.
- Community engagement strategy to address disparities in genomic research representation.

title: Evolutionary transcriptomics unveils rapid changes of gene expression patterns in flowering plants
abstract: The evolutionary history of angiosperms (flowering plants) is characterized by a highly accelerated rate of diversification. Although a number of hypotheses have been proposed to explain the ecological success and rapid rise of angiosperms, the molecular mechanisms underlying their speciation are only partially understood. In this study, we analyzed the developmental transcriptomes of seven angiosperm species spanning 160 million years of evolution. We demonstrate that angiosperm protein-coding gene expression patterns diverged rapidly. Particularly high rates of expression changes were found for genes that are involved in the response to endogenous and environmental stimuli, promoting broad ecological tolerances, adaptive evolution, and speciation. This study highlights how ecological and evolutionary dynamics are highly interrelated, shows that the rates of expression divergence differ from those previously described for mammals, and provides a comprehensive resource to perform cross-kingdom comparative studies of transcriptome evolution.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Analyzed developmental transcriptomes of 7 angiosperm species spanning 160 million years of evolution, revealing rapid divergence of gene expression patterns.
- Genes involved in response to endogenous and environmental stimuli show the highest rates of expression change, linking ecological adaptation to speciation.
- Rates of expression divergence in angiosperms differ markedly from those described in mammals, highlighting kingdom-level differences.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Comparative developmental transcriptomics across 7 angiosperm species chosen to span 160 million years of evolutionary history.
- Cross-kingdom comparison with existing mammalian transcriptome evolution data.
- Provides a public resource for comparative transcriptome evolution studies.

title: Genomic Next-Token Predictors are In-Context Learners
abstract: In-context learning (ICL) -- the capacity of a model to infer and apply abstract patterns from examples provided within its input -- has been extensively studied in large language models trained for next-token prediction on human text. In fact, prior work often attributes this emergent behavior to distinctive statistical properties in human language. This raises a fundamental question: can ICL arise organically in other sequence domains purely through large-scale predictive training? To explore this, we turn to genomic sequences, an alternative symbolic domain rich in statistical structure. Specifically, we study the Evo2 genomic model, trained predominantly on next-nucleotide (A/T/C/G) prediction, at a scale comparable to mid-sized LLMs. We develop a controlled experimental framework comprising symbolic reasoning tasks instantiated in both linguistic and genomic forms, enabling direct comparison of ICL across genomic and linguistic models. Our results show that genomic models, like their linguistic counterparts, exhibit log-linear gains in pattern induction as the number of in-context demonstrations increases. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first evidence of organically emergent ICL in genomic sequences, supporting the hypothesis that ICL arises as a consequence of large-scale predictive modeling over rich data. These findings extend emergent meta-learning beyond language, pointing toward a unified, modality-agnostic view of in-context learning.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Evo2 genomic foundation model exhibits in-context learning (ICL) capabilities analogous to language models, showing log-linear gains with more in-context demonstrations
- First evidence of organically emergent ICL in genomic sequences, supporting the hypothesis that ICL is a general property of large-scale predictive models
- Results suggest a unified, modality-agnostic view of in-context learning that extends beyond language to DNA sequences

## Methods Worth Noting

- Controlled experimental framework with symbolic reasoning tasks instantiated in both linguistic and genomic forms for direct comparison
- Evaluation of the Evo2 model (trained on next-nucleotide A/T/C/G prediction) at scale comparable to mid-sized LLMs

title: BioWorldModel: A Multi-Kingdom Trajectory Architecture for Genomic Prediction with Evolutionary Curriculum Learning
abstract: Genomic prediction models are trained on single species and ignore temporal dynamics -- assumptions that limit their biological scope. Here I present BioWorldModel, a unified architecture that predicts multi-trait phenotypic distributions across fungi, plants, and animals with a single set of parameters. The model introduces a scalable genotype encoder with organism-conditioned attention pooling, a four-channel biological memory system, and a Gaussian output head with diagonal variance parameterization. Trained jointly on five organisms spanning three kingdoms (S. cerevisiae, A. thaliana, D. melanogaster, O. sativa, Z. mays; 641 traits total), the model achieves organism-averaged R-squared = 0.821 (trait-weighted R-squared = 0.413) and substantially outperforms GBLUP, BayesB, Lasso, and Random Forest baselines trained per organism. These results demonstrate that genotype-to-phenotype mapping follows shared principles across kingdoms that a single model can exploit.
notes:
## Key Findings

- A single unified model predicts phenotypes across fungi, plants, and animals (5 organisms, 3 kingdoms, 641 traits) with organism-averaged R² = 0.821
- Substantially outperforms GBLUP, BayesB, Lasso, and Random Forest baselines that are trained per organism
- Demonstrates that genotype-to-phenotype mapping follows shared principles across kingdoms

## Methods Worth Noting

- Scalable genotype encoder with organism-conditioned attention pooling
- Four-channel biological memory system and Gaussian output head with diagonal variance parameterization
- Evolutionary curriculum learning for multi-kingdom training

title: Breeding ideotype maize with enhanced yield through genomics-guided pyramiding of favorable alleles
abstract: Breeding ideotype maize for high-density planting is crucial for increasing yield but is hindered by a limited understanding of plant architecture regulation. Here we uncover a prominent role for the accumulation of homozygous favorable alleles in genes regulating four principal shoot traits constituting a maize ideotype, and we identify and functionally validate eight architecture-regulating genes. Guided by genomics, we selected the elite hybrid Yufeng303 as a chassis to develop five improved parental lines by pyramiding favorable alleles of these eight genes together with genome-wide loci associated with these traits. These improved parental lines led to the creation of four new hybrids better suited for high-density planting, achieving 4.1–9.2% higher plot yields than Yufeng303 across eight environments. This study exemplifies the power of genomics-guided allele pyramiding in enhancing crop performance.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Identified and validated eight architecture-regulating genes controlling four principal shoot traits in maize ideotype
- Pyramiding favorable alleles of these genes created hybrids achieving 4.1–9.2% higher plot yields across eight environments
- Demonstrates practical genomics-guided breeding strategy for high-density planting maize improvement

## Methods Worth Noting

- Genomics-guided allele pyramiding approach combining GWAS-identified loci with functional validation
- Used elite hybrid Yufeng303 as chassis for developing improved parental lines

title: A genetic module boosts grain yield and nitrogen use efficiency by improving nitrate transport in maize
abstract: Although nitrogen fertilizer use has boosted crop yields, excessive application diminishes crop nitrogen use efficiency (NUE) and causes environmental problems. Therefore, increasing crop NUE is urgently needed for agricultural sustainability. Through a genome-wide association study, we identified a locus, NCR1 (Nitrate Concentration Regulator 1), that correlates with nitrate concentrations in maize root xylem. NCR1 encodes a MYB transcription factor that positively regulates the transcription of nitrate transporter NRT2.3 expressed predominantly in root xylem parenchyma cells. The NCR1–NRT2.3 transcription module responds to external nitrogen and controls nitrate translocation from roots to shoots. The superior NCR1−In allele with a 123-bp promoter deletion has decreased in frequency as nitrogen fertilizer use in China has increased. Overexpression of NCR1 or NRT2.3 significantly enhances grain yield and NUE in maize.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Identified NCR1 (MYB transcription factor) that regulates nitrate transporter NRT2.3, controlling nitrate translocation from roots to shoots in maize
- The superior NCR1−In allele (123-bp promoter deletion) has decreased in frequency with increased nitrogen fertilizer use in China
- Overexpression of NCR1 or NRT2.3 significantly enhances both grain yield and nitrogen use efficiency

## Methods Worth Noting

- GWAS on nitrate concentrations in maize root xylem to identify NCR1 locus
- Functional validation through overexpression and characterization of NCR1–NRT2.3 transcription module

title: Allelic variation at a single locus distinguishes spring and winter faba beans
abstract: Winter faba beans exhibit significant yield advantages over spring cultivars and hold promise for enhancing local protein production and agricultural sustainability. However, the threat of winter kill limits wider cultivation, and the genetics of faba bean winter hardiness remain unresolved. Here we develop a greatly improved faba bean reference genome and combine this with resequencing and phenotyping of winter and spring accessions to identify genetic determinants of winter hardiness. Genome-wide association analysis of frost tolerance traits identifies a major winter hardiness locus, the most strongly associated variant of which explains the vast majority of phenotypic variation and accurately differentiates between winter and spring types. Furthermore, we identify additional signals within the winter faba bean gene pool that could lead to further improvement of winter hardiness.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Developed a greatly improved faba bean reference genome
- Identified a major winter hardiness locus that explains the vast majority of phenotypic variation between winter and spring types
- Found additional genetic signals within winter faba beans for further hardiness improvement

## Methods Worth Noting

- Combined improved reference genome with resequencing and phenotyping of winter/spring accessions
- GWAS of frost tolerance traits to map the major winter hardiness locus
